narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection notice for our program. The admissions model we use evaluates your likelihood of passing the bar exam on your first try, based on key aspects of your academic history and profile. For you specifically, it predicted an 82.7% chance of failing the bar exam, which is a high-risk threshold that led to the automatic denial of your application to protect both you and our program's outcomes. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing, signaling weaker performance in later undergraduate years. Your Year 1 decile was also a notable drag, again landing in the lowest tier well below average, which reinforced concerns about consistent academic strength. Your LSAT score of 28 further nudged the model toward expecting challenges on the bar, as it sits notably lower than the average score of around 37 seen in successful candidates. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, while below the typical 3.21, actually worked slightly in your favor by softening the prediction a bit, and your middle family income aligned closely with averages without much sway either way. Overall, these elements combined to paint a picture riskier than most applicants, whose stronger deciles and test scores typically predict bar passage. We're rooting for you, and the good news is this isn't the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring part-time options or bridge programs that build proven success stories. Many students in similar situations have turned things around by targeting these areas, and we'd encourage you to reapply once you've addressed them. Keep pushing forward; your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer can make all the difference.
